lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: The English power is near, led on by Malcolm,

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: His uncle Siward and the good Macduff:

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: Revenges burn in them; for their dear causes

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: Would to the bleeding and the grim alarm

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: Excite the mortified man.

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Near Birnam wood

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Shall we well meet them; that way are they coming.

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Who knows if Donalbain be with his brother?

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: For certain, sir, he is not: I have a file

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Of all the gentry: there is Siward’s son,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: And many unrough youths that even now

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Protest their first of manhood.

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: What does the tyrant?

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Great Dunsinane he strongly fortifies:

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Some say he’s mad; others that lesser hate him

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Do call it valiant fury: but, for certain,

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: He cannot buckle his distemper’d cause

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Within the belt of rule.

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Now does he feel

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: His secret murders sticking on his hands;

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Now minutely revolts upbraid his faith-breach;

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Those he commands move only in command,

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Nothing in love: now does he feel his title

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Hang loose about him, like a giant’s robe

lines.speaker: ANGUS
lines.text: Upon a dwarfish thief.

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: Who then shall blame

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: His pester’d senses to recoil and start,

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: When all that is within him does condemn

lines.speaker: MENTEITH
lines.text: Itself for being there?

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Well, march we on,

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: To give obedience where ’tis truly owed:

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Meet we the medicine of the sickly weal,

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: And with him pour we in our country’s purge

lines.speaker: CAITHNESS
lines.text: Each drop of us.

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Or so much as it needs,

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: To dew the sovereign flower and drown the weeds.

lines.speaker: LENNOX
lines.text: Make we our march towards Birnam.

stage_directions.text: Drum and colours. Enter Menteith, Caithness, Angus, Lennox, and Soldiers.

glosses.definition: boys too young for beards — soldiers fighting for the first time
the hill-fortress in Perthshire, Macbeth's stronghold
reproach him for his broken faith
medicine for the ailing state — Malcolm as the remedy for the body politic
water the royal (Malcolm), the flower of sovereignty

annotations.title: 'Like a giant's robe upon a dwarfish thief'
annotations.body:
Angus's image for Macbeth's failing reign returns the play to its most sustained metaphor: kingship is clothing. Macbeth's title 'Hangs loose about him, like a giant's robe / Upon a dwarfish thief.' Cleanth Brooks's essay 'The Naked Babe and the Cloak of Manliness' reads this moment as the clothing-image system's summary statement.

The thief has stolen a robe cut for a bigger man. The longer he wears it, the more absurd he looks. The problem is not that Macbeth's ambition was too small (the play explicitly calls it 'vaulting'); the problem is that the office of king is not a fit: it was not cut for him, because kingship in this play's theology is a natural inheritance, not a position to be occupied.

The metaphor's last iteration will be Malcolm's coronation in 5.8, where the robe finds its body. The play's theological reading of kingship — inherited legitimacy restored after usurpation — is also a theatrical reading: it is about what fits.

trials.prompt: 'Like a giant's robe upon a dwarfish thief' summarizes:
options: The play's clothing-image system — Macbeth's kingship is a stolen robe that does not fit
Minute-by-minute defections reproach him for broken loyalty — his support is collapsing in real time
